hero_summary: Saba Zamzama is a relatively uncommon maqam that can be understood as a form of Saba in which the second degree is lowered by a semitone instead of taking the usual Saba interval. Because of that, it usually contains no quarter-tones and sounds closer to Kurd than to the traditional pure Saba profile.
short_identity: A maqam close to Saba in melodic logic, but structurally different from it because the second degree is lowered by a semitone. In practical form it begins from Saba Zamzama on Dukah (D), followed by Hijaz on Jaharkah (F).

quick_facts.ghammaz: Jaharkah (F), with possible further upper emphasis on Husayni (A) in some treatments
quick_facts.character: Close to Saba in melodic pathways, but auditorily closer to Kurd because quarter-tones are absent in its common form
quick_facts.common_start: It begins from Saba Zamzama on Dukah (D), then Hijaz on Jaharkah becomes clear, and an upper treatment on Husayni (A) may also appear

sayr.summary: Saba Zamzama is a relatively uncommon maqam in the Arabic repertoire. Theoretical descriptions present it as a form of Saba in which the second degree is lowered by a semitone rather than preserving the usual Saba microtonal interval. For that reason, in its common form it contains no quarter-tones and often sounds closer to a Kurdish field than to traditional Saba itself. Modern analytical literature also treats it as relatively later in taste and usage compared with core older Saba usage.
resting_tones: Dukah (D): the tonic in the common practical presentation
The second degree above the tonic: the decisive structural difference from ordinary Saba, because here it is lowered by a semitone rather than shaped by the traditional Saba interval
Jaharkah (F): an important upper point because it carries Hijaz in the common explanation
Husayni (A): an upper point that may support further Saba Zamzama treatment in some analyses
motion_notes: Its sayr retains some familiar pathways of Saba, but does not reproduce the exact same auditory effect
Because the second degree lacks the quarter-tone Saba inflection, the maqam moves more easily toward Kurd and Athar Kurd
It should not be performed as if it were pure Kurd, because its logic still belongs to the Saba world in phrasing and expressive route rather than in interval list alone
Its modern feel and ease on equally divided instruments make it distinct from classical Saba performance practice
common_path: Reveal the semitone-lowered second degree clearly
Move easily toward Kurd or Athar Kurd in treatment when needed
Return while preserving its tie to the world of Saba rather than to pure Kurd

common_modulations: The main distinction from ordinary Saba is that Saba Zamzama lowers the second degree by a semitone and usually contains no quarter-tones in its common form

It stands close to Kurd in overall sound, and some modern literature even describes it or relates it as a Kurdish-type Saba, yet it is not pure Kurd because its melodic pathways still retain a Saba relationship
There is also a strong relation to Athar Kurd in terms of ease of modulation, and modern analytical writing explicitly notes how readily Saba Zamzama may move toward Athar Kurd

Some educational explanations describe it simply as the form of Saba produced when Sikah is replaced by Kurd in the lower area, while modern analytical literature prefers the broader formulation: a form of Saba with a second degree lowered by a semitone
In practical analysis of some works, an upper Saba Zamzama on Husayni (A) may also appear, which shows that the brief scalar explanation alone is not always sufficient
